title: Legendrian Torus and Cable Links
speaker: Lisa Traynor
abstract: Legendrian torus knots were classified by Etnyre and Honda. I will explain the classification of Legendrian torus links. In particular, I will describe restrictions on the Legendrian torus knots that can be realized as the components of a Legendrian torus link, and I will give examples of Legendrian torus links that cannot be destabilized even though they do not have maximal Thurston-Bennequin invariant. Furthermore, I will explain that there are some smooth symmetries of Legendrian torus links that cannot be realized by a Legendrian isotopy.

proof_stages.label: Legendrian preliminaries and stabilization graphs
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the problem in standard contact R^3, explains front projections, Thurston-Bennequin and rotation numbers, and packages Legendrian representatives of a fixed smooth knot type into a stabilization or mountain-range graph.

proof_stages.label: Known torus-knot mountain ranges
proof_stages.summary: Using the established classification of Legendrian torus knots, positive torus knots are recalled to have a single peak while negative torus knots have multiple computable peaks, with tb and r determining the representative in these simple cases.

proof_stages.label: Torus-link tuples and realizability
proof_stages.summary: For an np,nq torus link, the classification problem is reformulated as deciding which n-tuples of component vertices in the corresponding torus-knot mountain range can occur, with a common-ancestor criterion for p≠1 and a separate subtler p=1 negative case.

proof_stages.label: Simplicity of realized link tuples
proof_stages.summary: Once a tuple is realizable, the theorem asserts that it realizes at most one unordered Legendrian torus link, so the component mountain-range data give an actual classification rather than only a list of possible invariants.

proof_stages.label: Component-permutation classification
proof_stages.summary: The talk then determines which smooth permutations of equal Legendrian components are induced by Legendrian isotopy: cyclic permutations always are, while noncyclic permutations are flexible in positive and non-maximal negative cases but obstructed at negative maximal-tb ancestors.

proof_stages.label: Cable-link extension from simple uniformly thick companions
proof_stages.summary: The torus-link results are extended to cables of a companion knot K when K is simple and uniformly thick, so that knowing K's mountain range and the slope q/p determines the cable-link classification and its permissible permutations.

proof_stages.label: Proof-method landscape leading to convex surfaces
proof_stages.summary: Special nonpermutation results can be proved in small cases by generating families or DGA/characteristic algebras, but the uniform all-p,q,n classification is routed through convex-surface theory and its dividing curves.

proof_stages.label: Convex-surface models and rigidity obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Positive ancestors are realized as ruling curves on standard convex tori and are therefore flexible under invariant-neighborhood isotopies, whereas negative ancestors lie on pre-Lagrangian tori in basic slices, where a reduction to a punctured annulus and Honda's uniqueness of minimizing configurations obstructs noncyclic permutations.
